sentences.text: Judith Goldstein.
Shapiro.
October 11, 1932.
In Vilna, Vilna, Poland.
Now it's Lithuania.
Chaim, Chaim Shapiro.
I think he was born in 1902.
He was an engineer, mechanical engineer.
And he loved music.
And he was just a very talented person, you know.
He did all kinds of things in the house, I remember, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like cigarette cases.
And he even tried to patent one cigarette case.
And he always-- I remember he made our sleds, you know, for my brother and myself, a lot of things.
Even at my bed, I had a speaker.
He connected the radio, and I had at my bed a speaker, which later had to be removed.
Because I was a kid with fantasies, and I saw too many things in that speaker.
[LAUGHS] So it was removed.
But he wanted to make sure that music was in the house.
My mother comes from [NON-ENGLISH].
And she got married in Vilna.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember her as being very-- also creative woman.
There was some embroideries in the house, some pictures that she had embroidered, even a-- a beautiful chandelier with fringes that hang over the dining room table, in sort of a-- almost this color.
I don't know, this is very coincidental.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] fans, you know.
The house was always in order.
And she was a wonderful housekeeper.
She also grew up from a poor family, so there were many children there.
And she learned how to sew.
And she used to do alterations for people.
And then when she came to Vilna, she kind of stepped up and used to get designs from Paris and do some replicas, you know, works of some very fine fashions.
And-- and she did this also I think not only for the money, but also for the love.
So I remember her making all my dresses.
And I would get up in the morning, and say, well what should I wear?
Because I had a closet full of clothes.
And it was nice.
You had any brothers or sisters?
I have one brother, yes.
And he survived the war.
He was born in 1926.
And he was in Buchenwald.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Of course, he was also in the ghetto.
Yes, I do.
Yes, I do, I have some memories.
Well, I recall the holidays were very festive.
My father was a very-- he was not so religious, but he was a very traditional person.
My mother was a little bit more traditional.
And she always made an issue about holidays.
And my father used to take us to the synagogue, like for [NON-ENGLISH].
I remember he took us once-- well, one memory I have of going to the [NON-ENGLISH], which was one of the major synagogues in Vilna.
Excuse me.
And that was fun, it was very-- a lot of fun.
Oh, yes.
They all flocked around our household.
[LAUGHS] I, I was a kind of kid who's happy.
And I would, like, play with anybody.
It was unfortunate that they-- some of them would reject me, because I was simply Jewish, even though I didn't look Jewish.
I had blue--
I had some-- no, they were not really the kind of friends who would like come knocking on my door.
But if I was out in the playground and in the sandbox or something, I would sometimes be attacked, verbally, as well as physically.
You know, they used to call me [NON-ENGLISH].
It means Jew fungus, or something like this.
Even though I looked less Jewish than they were, but you know, I guess it didn't matter.
I don't recall the year.
I don't know.
I could have been six or seven or eight.
Not that much.
You know, because even though for me the war started in 1941, at least that's what I remember.
But for the rest of Poland, you know, it started earlier.
But for me, it wasn't until 1941, when the-- when Germany occupied Vilna.
Actually, Vilna was-- changed hands many times.
And I do remember that Lithuania occupied part of Vilna, I think it was in 1940.
And in fact, for about one year, we went to a little town called Lida, where my father got a position in, in a factory as one of the engineers.
So we were away from home, from Vilna, for about one year.
And then we returned.
And I remember my father was-- like to him, Vilna, he kissed the grounds of Vilna.
He was in love with the-- with the city.
I guess they had you know, good memories of that.
Well, I don't remember too many things.
But what I do remember is that in the winter, I would skate to school.
I would put on my skates, because the layers of-- of the snow would pack up to such a degree where you could actually skate to school.
So I would put on my ice skates and go all the way to school and take them off at school.
And that was nice.
And then, it's funny.
Later on, when I was acquainted with music, and I listened to the 'Rustle of Springs,' by Sinding, I clearly saw the, the ice melting in front of my eyes, you know, I just saw it.
Because it's call to spring, you know.
It was interesting piece.
I always saw Vilna and the ice melting.
Well, it was a very big cultural, yes.
It had-- I know my parents used to attend concerts.
And especially when the Russians-- before the Lithuanians, the Russians were there for a little while.
And my parents went to concerts called [NON-ENGLISH], the Russian National Cossack type of dances.
And they loved it.
Not before the Germans came, no, no.
No.
In fact, the Russians offered my father a position in Moscow, which he refused.
I could have-- we could have avoided the whole war.
But then again, who knows what else would have happened?
Maybe I wouldn't be sitting here now talking to you.
But at least maybe he would have been alive.
He-- he was offered a position to Moscow, but he refused.
He was just so in love with Vilna.
He wanted to stay there, especially since he was away for one year.
And it's a good thing we did come back to Vilna.
Because in Lida, they literally wiped out every single Jew.
There was-- the smaller towns and villages were merciless.
My father's position.
That's made him.
He was-- again, he changed positions, so we came back.
But it wasn't that long.
We lived in a lovely apartment.
Although my brother went there a few years ago, and he saw it.
And you know, it didn't look as, as beautiful as it, as I remember it.
I think you know, a long time makes a lot of changes.
So but we did live on [NON-ENGLISH], which was right near a station with a, a terrace.
And my father always loved flowers.
So the terrace was filled with flowers, and lots of plants in the house.
And you mentioned before music.
Were you musically inclined or anybody else in the family was musically inclined?
Yes, yes.
My father's family, some of my cousins, particularly one I remember attended a Conservatory of Music in Vilna.
And she was studying the violin.
In fact, she met somebody there, a friend, a male friend, with whom, I guess, she had a romance.
She was a much older cousin.
And they fell in love, and they were either about to get married or married, I don't even remember.
My father loved music, he really did.
We had music in the house, phonograph and recordings.
I listened to Beniamino Gigli when I was just a little girl.
I was acquainted with some music already, not so much with the classics, more with the soloists, like opera.
Yes, I was exposed to it.
Because my father's family was not only musical, I had a cousin who was in the theater.
She was an actress.
And, she too-- I just liked it.
Even at nursery, my parents sent me to a musical nursery, where we used to express ourselves and play on instruments.
And I loved playing not only on percussion instruments, but I liked just the melodic instruments, like xylophones, where you could actually doodle around with a little melody, you know.
It was important to me.
No, this was-- this was the nursery school.
Then I started, I guess kindergarten.
And I don't even remember how many grades I made.
Because before I knew, the-- [SIGHS] the Germans were there.
And everything terminated.
It wasn't just-- it was an interesting day.
I have to tell you the story.
It was a Sunday morning.
At least I think it was on Sunday.
My father decided that he would take myself and my brother on a boat ride.
Vilna had water, surrounded by water, [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH].
It was a river.
And they used to have boats there.
And he decided that he would take my brother and myself on that day on an excursion.
My mother did not come.
And I still remember walking the streets.
We were-- I don't know how we got there, either by bus or I don't remember.
But we never got to really to the boat, to the marina, but we were en route to the marina.
And suddenly, people started to run in the streets.
And people were running into gates.
You know, when I say gates, I mean that most buildings had a gate.
And you entered the gate.
It's like sort of European style.
And they were running into the gates, off the streets.
And nobody knew what was happening.
My, my father didn't know.
And suddenly we began to hear bombs coming down, like rain.
And of course, then my father asked around.
He was speaking to people.
And within the hour, we knew that there was an invasion.
We didn't even know exactly who, at least I didn't [LAUGHS] really know.
Maybe my father did, I don't know.
And of course we never made it to the-- to the boat.
[LAUGHS] And we knew by then that we are in, in great trouble, especially the Germans.
Because the Jews knew what the Germans are going to do.
Already then I heard my parents discussing what the Germans did in the rest of Poland to the Jews.
No.
But we did get sometimes-- I remember once a young fellow rang our doorbell and asked to enter, you know.
He ran away from the part of Poland that was occupied by the Germans in 1939.
He ran away from home, and he knocked on our door.
And my mother was always a very generous woman.
She opened the door to everybody.
And I still remember how she fed him, and how he sat, bent over and cried, not knowing if he's ever going to see his parents.
And he told us that things are going on already, that the Jews are already being segregated, and they're being beaten.
And already it's-- it started, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] separating the Jews from the Christians.
And a lot of people were trying to get out, you know, from the areas.
And he was a fellow who just ran away.
He-- I, I think my mother fed him, maybe he even slept over.
I don't remember.
And then I never saw him again.
No, no.
That we didn't know.
That we didn't know until, until the Germans occupied Vilna.
Then what they did was if-- especially males, males were afraid to walk the streets.
Because they used to catch the males.
And sometimes these males were never seen again.
And sometimes these males were beaten and cut their hair, just random, you know, cut their at random, and they did return.
So people were afraid already to walk in the streets.
And then you couldn't walk already on the sidewalk, you had to walk-- And there was like a progression of, of hostilities developing toward the Jews.
And I think that the Jews were also not allowed to shop in certain shops, and not getting the right kind of food anymore.
You had like cards and rations and--
[SIGHS] It went--
It-- they came in, in June.
And in September, the ghetto was already created.
So within these three months, a lot of people didn't even enter the ghetto.
Vilna had about close to 80,000 Jews.
And each Jew, or family rather, got a special certificate, like [NON-ENGLISH].
And those people who lived in the poorer sections didn't even enter the ghetto.
The ghetto could-- could not accommodate 80,000 Jews.
So what they did, and a lot of my family disappeared right away.
We later on, we knew that they were slaughtered at Ponary.
Ponary, what was Ponary?
Ponary was a forest, outside of Vilna.
And they just picked a spot.
And they used to shoot them down like dogs in mass-- in mass graves.
My-- almost my entire-- my father's entire family lies buried in Ponary.
No.
It was not part the ghetto.
What they did was part of the ghetto, which they picked the poorest part in Vilna, like [NON-ENGLISH], I think it was [NON-ENGLISH].
And they had some names of streets there, very narrow little streets, and really a poor section.
And the Jews who lived and had businesses in those sections were actually removed overnight, all the families.
And they took them, I think to Ponary, and created space for the people who came in, like ourselves, who lived in the better and little more moderate section, not rich-rich.
I mean, we were not rich.
We lived very nice, you know, in a nicer area.
And they made room for us.
When we walked into the ghetto, we found beds are made, tables with-- with bites in bread.
They just came in, and removed them overnight.
They were even sleeping.
And they would like, ask them to leave, to vacate the apartment within minutes.
We heard this later.
Because some people ran away.
And it was difficult.
It was very difficult.
Yes, yes.
We had to leave our homes with just like what you can carry on your back, like a knapsack.
And you-- you took just a few items, and that was it.
And the interesting thing is, you know, we had a pretty nice apartment, with some interesting things in there.
Our neighbors who were not Jewish, couldn't wait for us to get out of the apartment.
They were inside our home, dismantling our possessions-- I remember this clearly-- before we even left the apartment.
They did apologize.
They said, well, [NON-ENGLISH], you are you leaving anyway.
And this, you know, why not?
Why not take it?
And they were really helping themselves to-- to our household.
And we left, we had to.
And you had to just the belongings on your back, and you left?
Just a few belongings on our backs.
I don't remember.
I think we had to meet someplace.
I don't-- I'm not quite sure how we got there.
My, my, my memory fails me.
I somehow remember the impressions it made on me, like the people coming into the house and, and you know, taking things apart while we were still there, and leaving anyway.
But we did get to the ghetto.
And I do remember entering the ghetto.
And each family was assigned not an apartment, [LAUGHS] a corner in a room.
If this was a room, each family had a corner.
And one in the center, if the room was large enough.
And we slept on the floor, of course, whatever we had.
And the kitchen was used by many people, I guess.
I don't remember how.
I guess my mother would know better if she was alive.
I don't remember.
Yes.
Yes.
I don't remember really.
I know that we couldn't walk on a sidewalk.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I don't remember how my mother got food.
I mean, not that I don't remember.
I guess it was not something I, I would know.
But it wasn't the same anymore.
And unfortunately, neighbors were not that friendly either to us.
I don't know.
I don't remember.
I know that my father worked.
And on a daily basis, they would exit the ghetto gate.
And--
No, no, women too, women too.
I think he worked on a plant again.
I-- I guess I never knew, or maybe I knew--
Right.
Maybe I knew, and I don't remember.
Or maybe I, I never knew.
I don't know.
All I know is that he worked.
And when people worked, it was an advantage for, for several reasons.
One, that they get a yellow certificate, it's-- it was called [SPEAKING GERMAN].
And based on that certificate, you were able to support or sort of allowed to live-- not support, but allowed to live-- a wife and children who are under 15.
And both my brother and I were, of course, under 15. d Any child that was over 15 was, was at risk.
Because they used to come into the ghetto and do like what they call a [GERMAN], like a cleaning.
And they would clean out those people who have no certificates, who have no job, and who are old, who are too old.
It means an important Jew.
A [GERMAN] means an important Jew.
He-- he's doing something important, you know.
And-- and I think it-- it applied mostly to, not only professionals-- my father was a professional-- but also to people who-- also from blue collar workers who did a, an important job, you know.
I don't really know what or who.
But anybody who was not important went to Ponary.
Because every time they cleaned out, they-- they would take people to Ponary.
Also later I heard-- and I don't know exactly when-- that there was another camp, Klooga, Klooga, or something like that.
They had people there.
And I think men, I think specifically men.
I don't think we knew it right away, no.
It wasn't until later, at least I don't know when I first found out about Ponary.
But I knew already while I was in the ghetto that many people are at risk.
And we all knew that eventually we will all be all at risk.
Because each time they made this [NON-ENGLISH], what I call it a cleaning, they were so brutal at the-- at the gate.
I have memories of pushing people and kicking them, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to such a degree where they-- if they shoved you, you didn't just fall on the floor.
You slid for-- for-- for many feet.
That's how severe the blows were.
And I remembered that.
The SS.
Germans.
No, I don't remember.
I remember people in uniforms, in green uniforms.
To me, they looked like they were Germans.
And we walked out from the gate, leaving those people behind.
My father had his mother there.
And for some sort of reason, she survived until the end.
I think my father hid her.
And I don't know exactly how.
No, no.
We were allowed to exit the ghetto.
Yes.
Because my father had the paper.
And we were kept for overnight, in some kind of a place-- I don't know what it was, what the name of it was-- where you just sat there and waited until the ghetto was emptied.
And then when you returned, the ghetto was emptier, and emptier.
Each time, it was emptier.
Yes.
But then the other hand, I also remember some nice things about the ghetto.

The people who did stay and who were still in the ghetto, since the element that stayed in the ghetto was a very creative element, there was actually a Philharmonic at the-- at the-- it was an orchestra.
And the conductor was Durmashkin, who later died in Klooga, I heard.
Because I met his sisters.
And they had a conservatory of music.
They had schools.
They weren't the kind of schools that we all picture now.
But it was schools, where they gathered children and at least introduced some things to them, some culture.
Yes, I did, yes.
No, it was both.
My mother-- my parents enrolled me at the Conservatory of Music.
It's there that I first touched a piano.
I started, yes, I started.
They had a very fine-- they had the best teachers.
These teachers were performers.
It's like studying, starting with people of Juilliard here.
The Conservatory of Music in Vilna was very known.
Besides Durmashkin there was [NON-ENGLISH].
She was a pianist, her husband was a cellist.
And there was an opera.
And you started sort of not just with a teacher who will teach you the keyboard, you started right away, right away with a bang.
And you learned everything-- theory, harmony, and the exposure of music.
